question_title: Will the interest in “trump signs executive order expanding workers access to retirement plans” change between 2026-05-07 and 2026-05-16 according to Google Trends?
summary: The executive order was signed April 30, 2026. The search term is a very specific news headline query. Calibrating from the observed May 2 value of 21 (vs peak of 100), the exponential decay rate is lambda≈0.78/day (half-life ~0.9 days). By May 7 (7 days post-peak), the Google Trends value is predicted to be near 0. By May 16 (16 days post-peak), it's definitively 0. When both values are 0 or within 3 of each other, the question resolves "Doesn't change." The main risk is that decay is slower than calibrated, leaving May 7 at 4+ while May 16 is 0 (→ Decreases). A resurgence in this exact search phrase is very unlikely.

factors.description: Calibrated decay from May 2 = 21 data point gives lambda=0.78/day, predicting both May 7 and May 16 near zero → both at 0 means 'Doesn't change'
Very specific 12-word headline query has zero baseline interest - once news cycle passes (7+ days), interest goes to 0 rapidly
Uncertainty in decay rate - if slower than calibrated (lambda ~0.3-0.4), May 7 could be 5-15 while May 16 is 0, producing a decrease of >3
Follow-up TrumpIRA.gov implementation news could theoretically drive searches, but would use different search terms than this exact headline
Google Trends noise at low volumes can create random small values (1-3 points), but unlikely to exceed the >3 threshold for resolution change

reasoning:
## Forecast Summary

**Question:** Will interest in "trump signs executive order expanding workers access to retirement plans" change between May 7 and May 16, 2026 on Google Trends?

### Key Findings:
- **The executive order was signed April 30, 2026** — creating a sharp spike in this very specific headline search query
- **By May 2, interest had already dropped to 21** (from peak of 100), indicating extremely fast decay (half-life ~0.9 days)
- **By May 7** (7 days post-peak), the Google Trends value should be near **0**
- **By May 16** (16 days post-peak), it will definitively be **0**
- When both dates show 0 (or within 3 points of each other), the resolution is **"Doesn't change"**

### Final Probabilities:
| Outcome | Probability |
|---------|-------------|
| **Doesn't change** | **85%** |
| Decreases | 12% |
| Increases | 3% |

The dominant scenario is both May 7 and May 16 at or near 0, resolving "Doesn't change." The main risk for "Decreases" is if the decay is slower than calibrated, leaving May 7 with residual interest (4+) while May 16 is at 0. "Increases" would require an unlikely resurgence in this exact 12-word headline search phrase.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will the interest in “trump signs executive order expanding workers access to retirement plans” change between 2026-05-07 and 2026-05-16 according to Google Trends?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43422)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves “Doesn't change” if the value on the timeline at [https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=trump%20signs%20executive%20order%20expanding%20workers%20access%20to%20retirement%20plans&date=2026-04-16%202026-05-16](https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=trump%20signs%20executive%20order%20expanding%20workers%20access%20to%20retirement%20plans&date=2026-04-16%202026-05-16) for 2026-05-16 is within 3 of the value at 2026-05-07. It resolves “Increases” if the value is more than 3 greater, and resolves “Decreases” if the value is more than 3 lower.
fine_print:
A script will be used to determine the resolution of this question. It will access the data on Google Trends using [SerpApi](https://serpapi.com/), and compare the height of the timeline for the two aforementioned dates. The specific python query will be structured as follows:
```
from serpapi import GoogleSearch

search = GoogleSearch(params={
  "api_key": API_KEY,
  "engine": "google_trends",
  "data_type": "TIMESERIES",
  "geo": "US",
  "tz": 0,
  "q": "trump signs executive order expanding workers access to retirement plans",
  "date": "2026-04-16 2026-05-16",
})
search.get_dict()
```
Note that there may be differences between the results returned by the API and the data appearing on the page. This seems to be due to the 'tz' parameter not having the intended effect in-browser. In this case, the API results will be considered authoritative.
Note that the precision of the timeline will be 1 day, so this will compare the overall interest for the whole day as determined by Google Trends.
Dates are determined in UTC.
If the data is no longer available, or the script fails, this question will be annulled or manually resolved by a moderator.

factors.description: Calibrated decay from May 2 = 21 data point gives lambda=0.78/day, predicting both May 7 and May 16 near zero - both near 0 means 'Doesn't change'
Very specific long headline query has essentially zero baseline - once news cycle passes, interest goes to 0 quickly
Uncertainty in decay rate - if slower than calibrated (lambda ~0.3-0.4), May 7 could be 5-15 while May 16 is 0, creating a decrease
Follow-up news coverage (TrumpIRA.gov launch, congressional debates) could create interest spike in May 7-16 - but would use different search terms
Google Trends noise at low volumes can create random small spikes (1-3 points), but unlikely to exceed the >3 threshold needed for resolution

input.anchor: Base rate for Google Trends headline queries: both values near 0 after news cycle = "Doesn't change" most likely (~80% for similar past questions)
input.assessment: The executive order was signed April 30, 2026, generating a spike in this very specific headline search query. The calibrated decay rate (from the May 2 = 21 data point) gives a half-life of ~0.9 days, suggesting both May 7 and May 16 values will be near 0 in the Google Trends resolution window (April 16 - May 16). When both values are 0 (or within 3 of each other at low levels), the question resolves "Doesn't change." The main risk is that May 7 retains some residual interest (4-10) while May 16 is at 0, creating a "Decreases" outcome. "Increases" requires a resurgence in this exact search phrase, which is unlikely 2+ weeks after the news.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. The exact decay rate - calibrated from a single data point (May 2 = 21), could be faster or slower. 2. Whether Google Trends shows non-zero values for very low volume queries vs reporting 0. 3. Whether follow-up news (TrumpIRA.gov launch) would drive searches for this exact headline phrase.
input.tool_audit: web_search: useful - confirmed EO signed April 30, 2026; research(): useful - confirmed news timing and provided decay model parameters; execute_code: essential - ran Monte Carlo simulations calibrated to the May 2 = 21 data point; fetch_url on Google Trends: failed (Google Trends requires JavaScript rendering)
input.process_reflection: The Google Trends fetch failed as expected - Google Trends requires JavaScript and is hard to scrape. The SerpAPI is mentioned in the fine print but isn't available as a tool. The research sub-agent provided good background on news query decay patterns. The key calibration point (May 2 = 21) from the question text was very useful for anchoring the exponential decay model. The simulation approach worked well for propagating uncertainty through the decay model.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

### Evidence Grounding
All factors are well-grounded in the research trace:

**Factor 1** (Calibrated decay lambda=0.78): The Metaculus question background explicitly states "The current value... (May 2, 2026) compared to the last 30 days is 21." The agent's code correctly computes lambda = -ln(21/100)/2 = 0.78, yielding near-zero predictions for both May 7 and May 16. ✓

**Factor 2** (Very specific long headline query): Supported by worldview research entry (google-trends-behavior-for-specific-long-news-sear-4f015698) which states λ ≈ 0.3–0.7/day for long-tail news queries, with return to near-zero within 7–14 days. ✓

**Factor 3** (Uncertainty in decay rate): The agent's own Monte Carlo with wider lambda uncertainty (mean 0.65, std 0.25) produced P(Decreases)=27%, directly supporting this factor. ✓

**Factor 4** (TrumpIRA.gov): Web search results confirm TrumpIRA.gov is mentioned in the White House EO URL, CNBC, and Yahoo Finance articles. The EO was signed April 30, 2026, confirmed by multiple sources. ✓

**Factor 5** (Google Trends noise): Supported by worldview research noting sampling artifacts and privacy noise for low-volume queries. ✓

### Double-Counting
Factors 1 and 2 are related (both support "Doesn't change" via decay mechanisms) but address different aspects — Factor 1 is a quantitative calibration from observed data, Factor 2 is a qualitative prior from research literature. Mild overlap but logits are moderate and the final estimate doesn't appear inflated by this.

### Wrong-Direction Factors
All factor signs are correct. Factors 1, 2, 5 support "Doesn't change"; Factor 3 supports "Decreases"; Factor 4 supports "Increases." All logically consistent with their descriptions.

### Resolution Criteria Alignment
The agent correctly engages with the ±3 threshold, compares May 7 vs May 16 values, understands the April 16-May 16 resolution window, and recognizes that daily granularity and SerpApi (not browser) are authoritative. ✓

### Probability Assessment
The agent's simulations produced a range:
- Tight calibrated model: 93.4% Doesn't change, 6.7% Decreases
- Enhanced Monte Carlo (wider uncertainty): 72.8% Doesn't change, 27.0% Decreases
- Scenario-weighted: 73.1% Doesn't change, 25.3% Decreases

The submitted 85%/12%/3% is a weighted blend leaning toward the calibrated model. My independent estimate based on the evidence would be approximately 78-82% Doesn't change, 15-18% Decreases, 3-4% Increases. The agent's 85% is at the high end of my range but defensible given: (1) the calibration point (May 2 = 21) directly observed for this exact query provides strong evidence of very fast decay, (2) at lambda=0.78, May 7 value is essentially 0, and (3) both May 7 and May 16 at 0 definitively resolves "Doesn't change." The ~5pp difference from my estimate is not large enough to constitute a meaningful error.

### Counterargument Quality
Strong. Identifies the single-calibration-point vulnerability, constructs a coherent alternative (lambda=0.35 → Decreases), and suggests a counter-forecaster's alternative distribution (60-70% Doesn't change). This demonstrates genuine self-examination.

### Confidence Assessment
0.45 confidence is appropriately calibrated for: single noisy calibration point, inability to fetch actual Google Trends data, and genuine model uncertainty between tight and wide decay estimates.

### Tool Audit
Agent claims "fetch_url on Google Trends: failed" — tool metrics show 1 call, 0 errors. The fetch technically succeeded (returned HTML content) but the content was a browser compatibility warning, not trend data. The agent's characterization is directionally accurate though technically imprecise. Not material.

### Worldview Consistency
The worldview research entries on Google Trends decay patterns are consistent with the agent's factors and support the same range of lambda values. No contradictions found.

### Comparable Past Forecasts
For similar questions (janet mills, multnomah athletic club, gauche), the agent assigned 65-76% to "Decreases" when the comparison dates straddled active decay (start date elevated, end date at baseline). The current question is structurally different: the calibration data shows the start date (May 7) is likely ALREADY at baseline, making "Doesn't change" dominant. The shift to 85% Doesn't change is justified by this structural difference.
